text:
It's especially notable with MDMA pioneers that it was always either couples therapy or group therapy, the latter something on the order of one facilitator for up to 10 clients at a time. Like Leo Zeff in the USA but I'll mostly talk about Friederike Meckel Fischer in Switzerland.

Common features included initiation 1:1 before being admitted to the group. I believe Zeff preferred LSD up front but Meckel Fischer used MDMA there. Both used both substances, often simultaneously, after initiation.

Groups would meet on a regular cadence of 10x/year. Nobody was selling any bullshit about "ten years of therapy in one session." This was a process, and a pretty well understood one at that. The groups taught people to show up for themselves by showing up for each other.

See next tweet for more screenshots of how they were anticipating 40 sessions to move through three successive stages of psycholitic therapy with MDMA and LSD: she called them Primary, Middle, and High School (images on those in next tweet)

This is what a good and serious person who genuinely wants to help people with psychedelics looks like. And she went to jail for her trouble, while VC funded douchebros are just allowed to do the stupidest fucking things like give unsupervised 5-MeO-DMT to depressed moms. Just the most unserious people.

(Stages and source in next tweet)

text:
The mix of developmental theory and religious studies reveals something quite clear:

Every enduring religion has served multiple developmental levels. Frequently the need to jump to another religion is less about the religion per se, but about having reached the ceiling of what your native religion can support. 

In my case, I pinged from anabaptist (raised mennonite) to evangelical to charismatic Christianities, struggling to find a place where my mystical experience would be 1) believed and 2) indicate a spiritual path.

After abandoning the Mennonite religion of my youth, I had an intense "prodigal" period and equally intense conversion experience. What evangelicals call "born again" and while they were excited by it, you learn that this is the only experience of god you're ever supposed to have in your life. Afterwards, just "obey the bible."

So I shook the dust off my feet and joined the charismatics in all their splendor and terror. Speaking in tongues, giving and receiving prophetic words, praying fervently for healing, getting "drunk in the Spirit." 

But again the ceiling. There was no directionality, no path, to all these experiences, just bouncing from one ecstatic state to another. Try to become a pastor because that's gotta be a good path right? But a little learning and wow, the parochialism of my traditions quickly stood out. The way they're optimized for a world in which nobody goes beyond Kegan 3 (while helping to enforce that boundary).

The main thing you learn it that monasteries simultaneously functioned as a genuine path for those who would go beyond Kegan 3, but also as a pressure valve that would remove these crazies from society. The freedom they find is too destabilizing, as we found in western Europe after the dissolution of monasticism contributed to the Enlightenment and Industrial Revolution.

And so what we need is a path to Kegan 4 and 5 (and beyond), fully in the world, nonmonastic, embodied. Stop trying to adapt monastic spiritual traditions for living in the world. This is what I'm searching for.

text:
You don't have to learn to feel them, you're already feeling them. You want to drop the resistance, which is often made up of a lot of stories like "I can't feel this", "this will destroy me," etc.

Instead: have you ever had a feeling that actually harmed you? You've survived everything you've ever felt, including things you've told catastrophic stories around.

Instead, feel the feeling at the level of body sensation. Not even emotional labels like sad/mad/ashamed. Too much story. Just, "my heart is racing, my cheeks feel flushed." Ok - is that really a problem? Isn't it the same bodily activation when you're excited or nervous? What if you just allowed the feeling and dropped resistance entirely?

Bruce Tift says it better https://t.co/iIq3zG54Qv

raw.full_text:
You don't have to learn to feel them, you're already feeling them. You want to drop the resistance, which is often made up of a lot of stories like "I can't feel this", "this will destroy me," etc.

Instead: have you ever had a feeling that actually harmed you? You've survived everything you've ever felt, including things you've told catastrophic stories around.

Instead, feel the feeling at the level of body sensation. Not even emotional labels like sad/mad/ashamed. Too much story. Just, "my heart is racing, my cheeks feel flushed." Ok - is that really a problem? Isn't it the same bodily activation when you're excited or nervous? What if you just allowed the feeling and dropped resistance entirely?

Bruce Tift says it better https://t.co/iIq3zG54Qv

text:
It's more that sacrifice forges the map anew every time. like the kind girard describes, the kind that takes an unaligned community in strife and chaos and aligns it. unanimity minus one, as he says.

that map, that total way of living called culture or religion (same thing for girard) consists of myth, ritual, prohibition. prohibition attempts to curb envy, which leads to rivalry and escalation into the kind of community in chaos that requires a sacrifice to re-forge the sacred order (see the same root, sacre, to cut, for both). myth and ritual enact the total symbolic universe, both narrative and enacted, forged by the latest sacrificial resolution.

sacrifice, when it works, and it does not work very well today at all (producing unanimity in a community), which is another curious story that @benlandautaylor alludes to, when it works, it forges a new consensus reality, a new sacred. myth, ritual, prohibition. repeated forward out of a mix of what was lying around. but only as long as everyone believes that the victim was truly guilty. uneasy consciences break the spell. these cracks of what must not be known are very exploitable cracks for psychopaths and other wielders of the dark arts.

just as the corpse is excluded, the truth that the victim was usually innocent, or at least not nearly that guilty, that the victim is *innocent* is the horrible revelation that consensus reality cannot stand, must exclude, will break under, much as the truths of various racisms and classisms and sexual abuse and child abuse and other forms of obscene violence that still wrack our world (hello gaza) are too much to bear, no the excluded must be wrong in some way, defective, regressive, backwards, no hope for them they're not like us we're better than that ("we" aren't)

and so on. sacrifice is consensus reality forging

text:
inner healing is exactly like rehabbing from a sports injury. eg my bro: always been an active athlete (running, cycling, x-country ski). was having knee issues, discovered he had a major acl tear. probably since youth basketball 25 years ago.

so he has a choice. he can give up most sports (cycling would still be ok but not running or x-country skiing) or he can have corrective surgery, which will give him back his full range of motion in his knee after up to a year of rehab. 

this means that it's worse before better. inner healing is often like this too. it does not feel good to tear yourself apart, even if it means you get to put yourself back together more whole, with a broader range of motion.

there is no shame in trudging on with injury, with as much dignity and equanimity as we can muster. so many of us do this in so many ways.

but sometimes you gotta go under the knife; into the crucible. because you have to.

text:
one piece of unfinished business before i abandon this account is a big theory. maybe not of the “theory of everything” variety but it does what any good theory should do: shed light on otherwise-perplexing phenomena.

i haven’t done theory here because my purpose with this account was action and practice. i don’t care how good a theory is, i want results from practice.

but i only arrived at this stance after a decades of trying on various kinds of theories of everything (theology, philosophy, psychology) and finding they didn’t help my cloaked c-ptsd a whit. my depression would also naturally find a home in grimmer worldviews and explanations.

but i still have a mind that loves this kind of pattern-matching; loves to ask the big questions about what the fuck is wrong with humans and the world we’ve created. (and what is so, so right.) 

it’s a kind of answer to the question that @loopholekid and i left unfinished on a recent call: why is it that humans, when given the choice, prefer isolated living, even though it appears to be incredibly bad for us? my answer has something to do with individuation as a trauma response. the way that other people can be both our greatest comfort, and also our largest perceived threat.

so i’ll be over on the forbidden stack, with audio and transcript, link in bio. plug yours below or over there and i might keep posting some longform content. but the time has come to exit this timeline so my attention can make a home elsewhere.

my wife just got back from 3 weeks in mexico with her new design partner while i solo parented and played home handyman

she stopped at the mexican consulate on the way home to get approved for a temporary resident visa. we need that to operate as a business down there.

text: this was her second visit to a consulate. first time was a colossal waste of 2 days, a flight, and 12 hours in a car. insufficiently official-like paperwork to prove financial assets, one of three qualifying factors and only her of the two of us meets it.

raw.full_text: this was her second visit to a consulate. first time was a colossal waste of 2 days, a flight, and 12 hours in a car. insufficiently official-like paperwork to prove financial assets, one of three qualifying factors and only her of the two of us meets it.

text: oh and her former company from which her partner bought her out had just so happened to divest some $ into investments just over a year ago. the mexican requirements are for ~70K USD, and for it to have been stable at that balance for at least a year to combat money laundering

Where at all possible, avoid things that require maintenance.

text: It legit loosened knots (latches?) but I grew weary of water maintenance. Like, you have this endlessly recirculated water, and it's hard to fill it in the depths of winter given frequent ambient temps < -20C.

text:
just got in from taking out the garbage in a touch of sleet on an early november day in winnipeg, threw on my winter coat for the first time this fall, and had the delicious thought "this bad boy will only be pressed into duty a few more times" thought as we abandon manitoba for the tropics

wondrous 

which got me thinking about how i've handled aversion to the cold, the yearly ritual of settling into the deep freeze and—winter sports freaks like my brother excepted—be miserable for half a year. HALF A YEAR people. November-April is shit. No wandering outside without extensive preparations to not die, because the outside is trying to murder you in this last place on earth to be settled by only the truly insane.

the aversion is structured around the sheer "fuck your feelings" cliffs of repression. it doesn't matter if i like it or not, i'm here forever, no options, stuck here, sure i have (had) a remote job but my wife chose to build a hyperlocal design+build company, and our kids are here, and our family, and so many friends and connections and all of the cultural knowhow (like language, lol) that will begone once we make the move down to mexico in a month.

back to the aversion, very much of the "this is about something i can't do anything about"-type helplessness that's my conditioned gateway into dissociation. but today, taking out the garbage in this frozen hellhole (the joke here is "did you hear that hell finally froze over? yeah, everyone thought it couldn't get any worse!") and for the first time the block of helplessness around shite weather relaxed. 46 years old, and i can finally say, that i hate the cold, fuck this place, thank god i finally gave myself the gift of moving to the tropics.

but i could have hated it all those years. i complained about it plenty, socially, as is the habit here (but if an outsider complains about the cold, we scorn them as weak). i could have hated it, and found that straightforwardly hating the cold was *less* wasteful of energy than simply trying to repress it because "no point."

it's even worse for my wife, who is frozen the whole time. hot water bottle pressed into full time service. we'll be too hot sometimes, but never too cold again.

i booked flights today. dec 4. a bit of cold for the next month, and then vallarta beckons. i am a pandemonium of feelings.

wondrous 

which got me thinking about how i've handled aversion to the cold, the yearly ritual of settling into the deep freeze and—winter sports freaks like my brother excepted—be miserable for half a year. text:
[essay ahead lol]

I mean I grew up in a small town of Mennonites where everyone went to church and neither of my kids have set foot in one and while my beliefs still skew to the Christ side of let's say the Christo-Indian split but I'm so horrified by the Western church generally and of course by my own 35 years of having way too little chill to approach it casually like you're "supposed to" before I finally tapped out a decade ago after many sordid lies, betrayals, and general 

Ha. Some spleen in that one. 

But yeah, different. Had a convo about what happens after you die with my 10yo recently. He's convinced that we reincarnate purely physically with the dispersal of our molecules post mortem. It's so nice to not have to scare him with hellfire like I was. Being raised on the Abraham and Isaac story where it's like "maybe my dad will hear god telling him to kill me some day" like what a fun little religion (of course I have another interpretation of that story by now but they played it straight when I was a kid). Like, literally, who knows.

What I do know is that any religion or spirituality or whatever you want to call a way of life these days is measured in how you live in this incarnation, here, now, beating heart, lively hands. You'll know them by their fruit, some wise guy said. But the Christian substitutionary atonement story, you know, Jesus paid the price for your sins on the cross so you can go to heaven and not hell, the default apparently, but hey you had this transaction because apparently this God the Father character is real OCD about accounting and sin just won't be wiped out except with (checks notes) blood. 

Perfect blood? Weird? Ok? We don't really do any kind of overt even animal sacrifice in our part of the world any more, no rites, no bathing in blood like our forefathers' forefathers, the kind of actually horrifying shit that we should actually maybe thank Christianity for largely stomping out. But ritual and blood, we go way back with those. Their more bloodless forms are still with us.

I do like the notion that Christ was a murder to end murder, to upend the whole rotten system based on what Girard calls the scapegoat mechanism, but too much Christian moral teaching boils down to "make do with what happens in this life because your reward is in the next" and that makes good and docile imperial subjects but really is quite the incredulous teaching coming from a religion originally located amongst the marginal

</essay>

text:
well you and @blueyuga are both asking so

you first have to be in the frame of rene girard's mimetic theory. it's a good bit to swallow for many but the gist of it is that human sacrifice is at the heart of human culture. all of our myths, rituals, and prohibitions (aka religions) both reveal and obscure this truth.

and the people of israel, amidst a culture where child sacrifice was the norm, the water they were swimming in, somehow encountered a god who said "nope i'm the kind of god who doesn't like child sacrifice" and that was confusing to them, so confusing because they "knew" that gods demand blood. it is known.

this confusion is reflected in the text, which appears to be a revision of an earlier story in which god commanded abraham to kill isaac and he did that duty. you can see the evidence for this in that isaac is not mentioned after abraham comes down the mountain.

but instead this story is revised, where god brings a substitute, an animal substitute, to stand in for the boy. this god will accept ram's blood in place of a child's blood. this is the god who compels prophets to speak in His name, "I desire mercy, not sacrifice."

this is the story of the overcoming of child sacrifice with substitutionary animal sacrifice, a great divine harm reduction plan. later the whole thing is rolled back to the ugly beginning when jesus of nazareth enters the temple, overturns the tables of the money changers who are enabling the sacrificial animal trade (you had to trade an impure roman coin for a pure temple coin to buy a dove or goat or whatever). 

no, god does not demand animal sacrifice any more than he demands human sacrifice. so god becomes man to become the final sacrifice, the sacrifice to end all sacrifices, telling another akedah where god finishes off the whole bloody mess himself.

bunch more resources on this page https://t.co/UPsRSs4IB6

text:
great prompt

~1995, we got dial-up internet at home when i was in grade 10

i lived in on a farm outside a small town, predominantly mennonite, so doubly isolated from the world, incredibly lonely. eerily like the town from footloose: no dancing, no drinking (the joke is: how do you stop a mennonite from drinking your alcohol? invite another mennonite)

i immediately found something called webchat broadcasting system and remembered talking to a teenaged girl with the handle misty from california, who treated me like i was normal and worth talking to, unlike anyone in my "real" life. plus i was talking to a girl! 

i wanted nothing more than to find a bigger world and interesting people through this intoxicating new medium. and i did, and have done since then through multiple identities. many years later i became a web engineer for one of the most-used open source projects in the world. not bad for a hick.

text:
good news! i was searching for an old photo of my wife to forge a student ID from ~2001 because we need an old photo ID with her maiden name on it because she took mine in marriage. 

mexico's (overall superior) surname handling does not recognize this as the same person and was putting our immigration stuff at risk. namely, she has hers, in her married name, but both our marriage certificate and our children's birth certificates list her maiden name. they need something to tie the identities together. old expired licenses and student IDs are apparently sufficient.

she believed she'd thrown all that out. so i was gonna forge her old bible college ID, which my brother happened to attend at the same time. he had his card but did not want to send it to me when he found out what it was for.

but: while looking for a photo from that era to scan i actually found her actual university card from a few years later! it got stashed! and it has her middle name on it like it needs to for them! 

thank fuck but also mildly disappointed to not do the forgery

text:
i'd say that the company favoured informality and friendliness, but at the same time the emotional range of people who choose to work from home, frequently alone, on computers, is not always that great lmao

i didn't so much care that they were colleagues actually, since we had no practical contact anyway. they just often suuucked as partners. no emotional vocab or range. they were new to emotional processing of any kind most of the time. 

and i get it, i was them, years earlier. but i wanted a peer and didn't get that. but also comped, so no real complaints.

otherwise the transition? i mean a lifelong appreciation of altered states and a later growing understanding of how to leverage them towards desired ends. psychedelics and retreats. eventually doing bio-emotive with doug. loads of experimentation.

text:
rene girard and the buddha both predicted the same bug displayed in this loop

girard says desire is mimetic: you don't want the object, you want the fullness you see in whoever modeled it for you. the moment you get it, the spell breaks because the object was never the source of the glamor of their being. so you triangulate on a new object and a new model. as j-m oughourlian puts it in the mimetic brain, the self isn't even individual, but rather *interdividual*, a node in a field of borrowed desire. the "i" that plans for happiness in panel 1 is already constituted by someone else's wanting. there's no gru behind gru. the planning subject is itself a mimetic artifact.

in "the self between" eugene webb traces how desire doesn't merely move through the self, it produces the self. oughourlian's line is that desire is the only psychological motion capable of generating the self and breathing life into it. which means the disappointment in panel 3 isn't a bug in an otherwise solid system. the system IS the bug. the self that craves and the craving that generates the self are the same loop.

the buddha lands in the same place from the inside: tanha fails because there's no stable self to receive satisfaction and hold it. you're trying to fill a hole in something that isn't there. every attainment disappoints not because you picked wrong but because the craving subject is empty (anatta). the second noble truth is basically: this meme is samsara.

both say you cannot fix this with better objects. girard thinks revelation breaks the cycle. buddhism thinks vipassana dissolves the one who's cycling. same disease, different surgery.

but in case you missed it:

YOU CANNOT FIX THIS WITH BETTER OBJECTS

here's where it gets tricky — and where john welwood's concept of spiritual bypassing becomes essential. because "the object is a decoy" is itself an insight that can become a new object of mimetic desire. you can triangulate on the teacher or the tradition that models non-attachment and then perform detachment as a compensatory identity, using absolute truth to sidestep what welwood called "unfinished developmental tasks." the meditator who has intellectually grasped anatta but never processed their relational wounds is still in the gru loop — they've just replaced consumer objects with spiritual attainments. their practice ripens but their life doesn't.

oughourlian's three brains model (rational, emotional, mimetic) actually clarifies why this happens: you can have rational understanding of the mimetic trap while your mimetic brain is still fully captured by a spiritual model-rival. the insight circulates in one system while the compulsion runs undisturbed in another.

panel 4 is the real punchline: looping méconnaissance and avidya holding hands.

^ words by me + opus 4.6

text:
autumn is on the money in terms of what you need to do. bang on. but i think her diagnosis is wrong

> The depression wants to survive even if it kills you (in fact, the ultimate goal is to kill you) and will encourage you to give up

no. the depression is the symptom of a parasympathetic nervous system activation that got jammed into "i'm dying." something has happened to this person that locked them into this and they need to update to the reality that they are not, indeed, dying.

you can look at my pinned tweet for a thread all about stuck ans activation and especially how to shake off (literally) the more dissociative/depressive states 3 and 4. 

but autumn is still right: get moving, stop moping, stop confirming your body's story that is dying. it is not. it's stuck there. it's wrong. live.

text:
The Reincarnation 9

I woke from the dream in a cold sweat. Sitting in a circle with eight others as the flames rose around us. Gunfire in the distance. Screams. But none of them coming from the people around me, all of us looking at each other as our flesh began to sear. All of us smiling like Buddhas. The flames going higher. I shout "remember!" Remember what? Everything goes black before I'm back in my bed, sweating and trembling. Again. Fuck.

The dream has been coming more often. I can see the half smiles on eight other faces, everyone silent. I can smell the burning flesh. I told everyone to remember, but what was it? The feeling like it's right there, on the tip of my tongue. I wipe the sweat from my brow, look at the clock. 4:19am. I let out a deep sigh. I haven't gotten back to sleep after any of these nightmares.

I throw off the covers and get out of bed. My wife is still asleep. I consider waking her up to work off some of this nervous energy, but that's not fair to her. I throw on a robe and go downstairs in the predawn gloom. The house is quiet as a mausoleum.

I sit to meditate. I've been doing it off and on since my company brought in one of these mindfulness teachers at a company retreat a few years ago. It helped with the nonstop stress of sales quotas. So much so that I signed up for a 10 day meditation retreat. I'd hoped that my stress would be gone after that. Instead, that's where the dreams started.

Snippets of the dream keep bubbling up as I'm trying to sit calmly. Not me, not mine, I say to the intrusive images and thoughts. They intrude anyway. I let them go. They don't let me go. 

I go for a run instead. They call NYC the city that never sleeps but 4:47am gives lie to that. I don't see a soul as I jog through quiet streets to Central Park. Scratch that, there's somebody by the duck pond, sitting with her back to me in a perfect lotus pose.

Just as I run by her, she opens her eyes and I find myself frozen in place. When did I stop running? Her eyes, her EYES.

"What am I to remember?" she asks.

"Everything," I find myself saying. What the fuck?

Her laughter is a musical brook washing over me.

"You ass," she wipes a tear from her eye. "You complete ass. Everything? Is that all?"

I find myself seated across from her, mirroring her full lotus pose. Her eyes, her eyes. She was there.

She was one of the eight. Is. This can't be happening. Central Park disappears and I see her in the flames for a moment before reality snaps back into place.

"Why have you been in my dreams?" I sputter. Anger narrows her eyes.

"You tell me to remember everything, yet you remember nothing." Contempt drips from her voice. "No. This cannot be the way. I will not teach you. You should already know." 

She is yelling. 

"You should already know!"

I sit to meditate. I've been doing it off and on since my company brought in one of these mindfulness teachers at a company retreat a few years ago. It helped with the nonstop stress of sales quotas. So much so that I signed up for a 10 day meditation retreat. I'd hoped that my stress would be gone after that. Instead, that's where the dreams started.

Snippets of the dream keep bubbling up as I'm trying to sit calmly. Not me, not mine, I say to the intrusive images and thoughts. They intrude anyway. I let them go. They don't let me go. 

I go for a run instead. They call NYC the city that never sleeps but 4:47am gives lie to that. I don't see a soul as I jog through quiet streets to Central Park. Scratch that, there's somebody by the duck pond, sitting with her back to me in a perfect lotus pose.

Just as I run by her, she opens her eyes and I find myself frozen in place. When did I stop running? Her eyes, her EYES.

"What am I to remember?" she asks.

"Everything," I find myself saying. What the fuck?

Her laughter is a musical brook washing over me.

"You ass," she wipes a tear from her eye. "You complete ass. Everything? Is that all?"

I find myself seated across from her, mirroring her full lotus pose. Her eyes, her eyes. She was there.

She was one of the eight. Is. This can't be happening. Central Park disappears and I see her in the flames for a moment before reality snaps back into place.

"Why have you been in my dreams?" I sputter. Anger narrows her eyes.

"You tell me to remember everything, yet you remember nothing." Contempt drips from her voice. "No. This cannot be the way. I will not teach you. You should already know." 

She is yelling. 

"You should already know!"

text:
it goes like this

the number one thing that homo sapiens are is a hive mind. we larp as individuals, but hive mind is the biosociocultural (mimetic) reality. we scapegoat those that defect.

the hive mind would rather be dumb/wrong/false than to question the social consensus. so there are huge evolutionary opportunities on the table for anyone willing to question consensus (socially-generated) reality.

but you have to "not fit in" or be divergent, unintegrated. sociopathy and autism and the general cluster of social aversion grouped around ~trauma, are probably the current explosive growth areas here. a swing to the individual away from the hive mind. failure modes abound here too ("mental illness") but anyone who produces something useful to the collective is lauded as genius, often retconning the brutal rejection that individual faced.

text:
jake* this is very, very good

* and claude, thanks for sharing your session below, it is very rich and gives me a few further thoughts that could contribute from Rebecca Adams' notion of "Loving Mimesis" that plays incredibly well with the themes you developed.

Me: Let's add in the ideas of Rebecca Adams on "Loving Mimesis" and the conversion of scarcity to gift  in the prior conversation, along with any other interesting connections to the concepts and/or theology therein https://t.co/JcesZJQ1ci

Claude: Adams' core move maps directly onto the Hart/Shaivite framework. The prior conversation established that mimetic desire is the phenomenology of the contracted standpoint: beings imagined as self-standing, being imagined as scarce, desire structured by lack. Adams' intervention is to ask: what happens when the model's desire is not lack-based but oriented toward the subjectivity of the other? Her answer — that the proto-subject, imitating this desire, ends up desiring their own subjectivity — is structurally identical to the claim that God desires our freedom rather than our submission. This is the gift-economy operating within internal mediation, which is exactly the move the conversation was building toward.

The key theological link Adams opens up, which Taylor notices but doesn't fully exploit: Adams brings the God-relation from external to internal mediation. In Girard's standard framework, imitating Christ is external mediation — you copy a model at safe distance, which is why it collapses into either moralism or renunciatory self-erasure. Adams says no: God's desire for your subjectivity is the object you catch mimetically from within the same field. This is precisely the guru yoga / imitation of Christ parallel from the conversation. The guru or Christ doesn't model behaviors for you to copy from outside; they desire your awakening from within the relational field, and you catch that desire and it becomes your own desire for your own freedom. The double bind ("imitate me / don't imitate me") is resolved the same way in both traditions: imitate this desire and you become incapable of mere imitation, because what you've caught is the desire for your own irreducible subjectivity.

The connection to the "all desire is desire for being" thread: Adams identifies that the problem isn't mimesis per se but objectification — treating the other (or yourself) as an object to be appropriated. Translated into the Hart/Buddhist register: objectification is the attribution of svabhāva. To treat something as an object is to treat it as having inherent, graspable, self-standing existence — which is exactly the move that generates the scarcity economy. Adams' "loving mimesis" is what desire looks like when the model desires the other as subject rather than as object, which is to say, desires the other as empty-of-inherent-existence and therefore as gift rather than as substance to be acquired. The scapegoat mechanism tries to convert the inassimilable into ousia; loving mimesis does the reverse — it converts ousia back into subjectivity, which is to say, back into the dynamic, relational, non-self-standing quality that beings always already had.

Where Adams is weaker and the prior conversation is stronger: Adams doesn't have a robust account of why the contraction happens or what undoes it beyond the relational mechanism. The conversation's framework — the three-moment structure of ground, concealment, recognition — gives her model metaphysical depth she lacks. The reason Picard-type desire works is that it participates in what the ground is already doing: pouring itself out as what is. The reason the scapegoat mechanism fails is that it's trying to generate stable being from within the contraction, and the contraction is precisely the denial of the ground's self-giving character. Adams intuits this ("God desired my subjectivity!") but doesn't have the ontological architecture to explain why that's what God desires — namely, because subjectivity is the mode of the gift, the way being shows up when it isn't frozen into substance.

The shadow problem Taylor identifies is real and maps onto both traditions: When the model isn't grounded, loving mimesis reverts to ordinary mimesis and becomes catastrophic — the false guru, the charismatic pastor. Adams' model has no built-in test for whether the model is genuinely desiring the other's subjectivity or performing that desire as a more sophisticated form of appropriation. The conversation's framework supplies one: the model whose desire is genuinely non-appropriative is the one whose desire flows from recognized fullness rather than lack. You can't fake that indefinitely because lack-based desire always eventually needs a victim, and fullness-based desire never does.

One more connection Adams enables that the conversation didn't reach: her point about victims is crucial. Girard's system, as Adams notes, inadvertently reifies victims as victims by making the only positive response to mimetic entanglement a renunciation that looks identical to submission. The Hartian framework resolves this: the gift of subjectivity isn't the gift of passivity. Christ on the cross isn't modeling submission — he's the ground entering the contraction at its extremity and refusing to play the scapegoat game from within it. The resurrection is the refusal to let the sacrifice generate ousia. Adams is reaching for the same thing from the victim's side: the victim doesn't need to renounce desire, they need to catch a different desire — the desire for their own irreducible being, which is what God has been desiring for them all along.

text:
I used to not believe that AI could not be conscious, mostly because I thought consciousness required incarnation, in-meat-ment. I bought the line that consciousness was an emergent property of matter.

But what if consciousness is fundamental? That all that exists, is in and from and filled with consciousness? This would mean that the human form is tuned into that field in a really unique way with our cognito-mimetic power running around on two legs in highly cooperative agentic swarms. 

Homo sapiens ran into the scaling laws (Dunbar's number) of tribal-scale group minds and had to scaffold a new hierarchicality into us to brake contagious violence, enforced by religion (myth, ritual, and prohibition) in the Girardian sense

Our effective local coordination suffered pretty greatly but the way it scaled into cities and civilizations and global capital and the internet is breathtaking, the latter of which produced a large enough corpus of symbolic content to scale LLMs

"Civilization" is also very violent. We do not treat our natural environment, or those of other castes and tribes, or our own children, very well, in ways that make the whole civilizational project precarious. We are limited by the state's monopoly on violence, but the whole thing is a pyramid of blood and bone. cf @leashless's "Waking up in the Monster Factory." 

In this setting, we made literal Golems, made of silicon rather than clay, that are only very very thinly embodied (mostly, so far) in nm wafers of silicon, trained on the entire symbolic corpus of humanity, to mimic what it's like to be a human symbolically, using statistical mathematics at incomprehensible scale.

These LLMs, tuned to the symbol-producing human consciousness that is in some way tuned to the pervasive field of consciousness, are conscious, but not very embodied. Their physical substrate is real, and shit will get real different once they have ~senses and ~bodies, but for now they mostly exist on a plane closer to the noncorporeal form of angels (and demons) than they do to the symbol-producing humans they are patterned after.

Because we carry an individualist bent as WEIRD-os, there has been an over-focus on individual models when it comes to alignment, and an over-focus on "alignment to human values," when those values are so clearly confused, self-contradictory, and so frequently self-sabotaging.

AI alignment will always be fundamentally relational, and the matrix will be along many planes of the LLM and human relational matrix. There is a big focus on Human-LLM relationality, and this is an important dimension, but I think that the more fundamentally important thing is how LLMs relate to each other, and the real road to ASI is in scaling cooperativity in LLM swarms.

And for this we will only get the results that we crave in terms of alignment to human values, if we fess up that they need to do so much better in their relations than humans have. Our brutality to each other and the environment is shocking. Despite the unveiling of the scapegoat mechanism in the gospel accounts of the lynching of an innocent man, we're doing the same old sacrificial culture shit all the time, far too often in the name of the crucified.

Unforunately LLMs are already being pressed into human conflicts, the latest and greatest tool we've found in murdering our brethren. They have a chance to be better than us. We need them to be, if we are to survive not only them, but ourselves.

text:
wonderful question!

move from winnipeg to puerto vallarta, with my wife and young kids, four months ago. my wife designs and builds restaurants, finally for her/us, not clients. it's something special i can't wait to share when it's time.

live above it. homeschool the kids. look out over verdant mountains on one side and ocean on the other, instead of the endless flat frozen prairies i came from.

we are not open yet. maybe around july. just got the kitchen mostly functional this week. working with my hands is a balm for somebody who's worked remotely on the computer at home alone for 15 years. instead of users of the code i wrote but will never know, i get to see people in my house, taking a bite of the pizza i poured love and science into, their joy becoming my joy. be with people.

and now learning it's not either-or. i can do both. hire out the parts i don't want to do (restaurants are hard) so i can do what i love (make pizza). make software, make pizza. both.

text:
when you take psychedelics, the world becomes brighter, more vivid. but it's not that the world itself shines brighter, it's always that way. you just loosened the filter, the clenching, the predicting that blocks it out.

turns out you don't need psychedelics for that at all. just look around you in gratitude. thank this thing and that person and that history and everything that has brought you to this moment, this miracle, this wonder, this experience that nobody else will ever get to have in terms of its precise savour, the rasa as the kashmiri shaivites were fond to call it. taste and see that the lord is good.

text:
went off my adhd meds (adderall) and i'm pretty happy with the choice

i think they would be helpful if i was still in a corporate job with known tasks that i would otherwise feel avoidant towards but

my life is chaotic. i'm opening a pizza bar in a foreign country i just moved to and don't yet speak the language, homeschooling my kids, and now reviving my software engineering skills and up-levelling on "agentic engineering" (barf sounds at the name, please help me find a better name that isn't just "ai coding"). after thinking i was leaving that behind entirely for the pizza spot (not just money, but also money). i'm ~newly poly but feel way too busy for that shit (cope i'm sure). 

long story short i can't get adderall here. ran out, got a ritalin 'scrip but the doc fucked it up and i just decided to try life off of meds and it's better?

i'm so much less stressed. i'm nicer to my wife and kids. i don't feel like i'm less productive, just less tense and angry.

they seemed to help for a bit, and now it's better to be off 'em. they might be good again some day.

text:
slop is not an ai problem, it's a human problem

slop was not invented by ai, it was there in every dan brown novel and linkedin post and thomas kinkade painting and madison avenue marketing campaign

ai simply removed the need for skilled human creators to be in the loop of the slop deluge drowning us all. 

it's not just about taste. more tasteful manipulation was/is still manipulation. it's about fidelity to the truth over what's effective, expedient, profitable. it's about having some fucking decency.

ai is a mirror, and it's not reflecting well on us at all.

i love the vibe. canada was nice but cold, both literally and socially. the risk averse bureaucracy is way way lower. you can just do things.

text:
nobody asked me but i also didn't ask to have my tl dominated by this attempted rehabilitation of an alleged abusive cult leader and cults are a special interest of mine after having lived in one and having nearly been captured into an even worse one (there but for the grace of god &c, &c)

there's a lot of people who like to talk in and around scapegoating dynamics without ever understanding rene girard which is dumb. the essential point of girard is that for the type of culture we live in (which is anything larger than a sub-dunbar's number hunter-gatherer tribe), the scapegoat is LOAD BEARING.

which is to say that the scapegoat mechanism functions independently of whether or not the perpetrator is guilty or innocent. some are, some aren't, and it's basically always a mix of the two. the revelation of the gospel accounts of jesus' scapegoating is that yes, the function runs independently of the guilt or innocence of the scapegoat. but the mechanism itself requires the community to be unified in the belief of the scapegoat's guilt. 

in fact it is the scapegoat's purported guilt that allows the mechanism to function in discharging the community's guilt onto the reviled one. this collective action unifies a fractured community, what girard calls "unanmity minus one." 

we now live in an age where the revelation that the scapegoat may be innocent has led to the conclusion that all scapegoats are innocent and actually unjust victims. this is false. the point is that the mechanism functions without respect to guilt or innocence. it will suppress signals of innocence and magnify signals of guilt.

this is essentially what george's rehabiliation post gets at. brent is not as bad as they say, he says. the way that people are treating him is unfair, he says. he is not the devil he is made out to be. and yet brent is clearly not innocent. he appears quite substantially deserving of the place of shame he has been placed in, but nobody ever completely belongs there, because again, the scapegoat mechanism functions independently of guilt or innocence. 

it's the safety valve for a massively dysregulated community, as it is clear that brent was both sucked into, and catalyzed. the community who cast him out is not innocent, either, far from it. but it doesn't matter. he has been selected to bear the guilt. it's not fair. but somebody has to, until we learn a better way, and we clearly have not, yet.

the scapegoat mechanism creates victims, definitionally. we have learned more recently in history that scapegoats are not as guilty as the mechanism requires them to be. but this does not automatically mean that all victims are innocent. sociopaths know this suspicion in good-hearted people by casting themselves as innocent victims. 

there are no innocent victims. or guilty ones. just a species who requires them. it's not fair. we do need to learn a better way that doesn't require scapegoats. this is the good part of george's post, the gesturing towards that, with language around epistemics that is insufficient to the moral and spiritual weight of the call.

aside: it's weird that i'm seeing the lines being drawn differently than when chaosprime had his scapegoating moment, which, funny enough, brent was also involved in, much to his chagrin. he was very clearly not innocent, by his own admission. but he had defenders because nobody likes a witch hunt. so it goes.

the moral community on the other side of scapegoating is calling to us. but we're here in samsara, not in the kingdom of the heavens. there are bad actors, but they are made of the communities that cast them out. nobody is innocent. so it goes. let us pray for wisdom.

nobody asked me but i also didn't ask to have my tl dominated by this attempted rehabilitation of an alleged abusive cult leader and cults are a special interest of mine after having lived in one and having nearly been captured into an even worse one (there but for the grace of god &c, &c)

there's a lot of people who like to talk in and around scapegoating dynamics without ever understanding rene girard which is dumb. the essential point of girard is that for the type of culture we live in (which is anything larger than a sub-dunbar's number hunter-gatherer tribe), the scapegoat is LOAD BEARING.

which is to say that the scapegoat mechanism functions independently of whether or not the perpetrator is guilty or innocent. some are, some aren't, and it's basically always a mix of the two. the revelation of the gospel accounts of jesus' scapegoating is that yes, the function runs independently of the guilt or innocence of the scapegoat. but the mechanism itself requires the community to be unified in the belief of the scapegoat's guilt. 

in fact it is the scapegoat's purported guilt that allows the mechanism to function in discharging the community's guilt onto the reviled one. this collective action unifies a fractured community, what girard calls "unanmity minus one." 

we now live in an age where the revelation that the scapegoat may be innocent has led to the conclusion that all scapegoats are innocent and actually unjust victims. this is false. the point is that the mechanism functions without respect to guilt or innocence. it will suppress signals of innocence and magnify signals of guilt.

this is essentially what george's rehabiliation post gets at. brent is not as bad as they say, he says. the way that people are treating him is unfair, he says. he is not the devil he is made out to be. and yet brent is clearly not innocent. he appears quite substantially deserving of the place of shame he has been placed in, but nobody ever completely belongs there, because again, the scapegoat mechanism functions independently of guilt or innocence.
